source_paper_id: A New Metric for Integrative Analysis of Movement.txt
paper_title: The Intensity Inequality Index for Physical Activity: A New Metric for Integrative Analysis of Movement
key_methodology: PART 1 — METHODOLOGY: The Intensity Inequality Index (I≠) is adapted from an econometric measure (the Gini coefficient), functioning as a "Gini coefficient for movement." The algorithm calculates I≠ using a graphical method on plots of cumulative time versus cumulative intensity level, quantifying the inequality in distribution of time spent across a range of activity intensity values. Parameters include: 24-hour activity traces recorded via wearable accelerometer sensors, a sample of 58 children aged 7–11 years, and comparison groups of typically developing children versus those suspected of having developmental coordination disorder (DCD). Hypothesis testing of I≠ is performed at significance level α = .05 with statistical power of 80%.
primary_finding: PART 2 — FINDING: The I≠ metric provided high statistical confidence with low sample numbers: P < .05 for n ≥ 30. When differentiating between groups (typically developing children vs. those suspected of DCD), I≠ halved the sample size required for a statistical power of 80% at α = .05, compared to the alternative metrics of intensity gradient or log ratio of minutes at low and moderate to high intensity.
raw_quote: When differentiating between groups, I≠ halved the sample size required for a statistical power of 80% at α = .05, in comparison to the alternative metrics of intensity gradient or log ratio of minutes at low and moderate to high intensity.

paper_title: Creativity in sport and dance: Ecological dynamics on a hierarchically soft-assembled perception-action landscape
key_methodology:
PART 1 — MECHANISM (causal chain): Creative behaviour in complex neurobiological systems emerges through the interaction of three nonlinear properties: cause-effect non-proportionality, parametric (constraint) control, and multi-stability. Constraints break system symmetry and ergodicity, producing phase transitions that create high barriers on the potential landscape, confining available actions within an internal space. This generates a rugged, hierarchically soft-assembled potential landscape with many nested metastable minima. Within this landscape, the performer-environment system undergoes exploratory dynamics — hopping from one attractor basin to another, driven by both deterministic constraint structure and stochastic agitation from the interaction between the performer's intention to move and unpredictable physical and informational constraints. Larger hops over higher barriers produce larger reconfigurations (more novel movement forms), while smaller hops produce incremental variations. The causal chain is: interacting constraints (task, individual, environment) → symmetry-breaking and ergodicity-breaking → soft-assembled hierarchical potential landscape with nested metastable minima → stochastic-deterministic exploratory dynamics (hopping between attractor basins) → reconfigurations of action patterns at multiple time scales → emergence of novel and functional movement forms (creative products).

PART 2 — BEHAVIORAL PREDICTION for movement data: In a contact improvisation dance session, the average dynamic overlap <qd(t)> between movement configurations will not decay exponentially to zero, but will instead show a power-law initial relaxation followed by a plateau at a value significantly above zero (predicted plateau ~0.45), indicating that the system explores correlated movement configurations within a confined but large region of state space (broken ergodicity). The hierarchical PCA of movement configurations will reveal a nested attractor basin structure with a small number of slow collective variables (primary PCs) accounting for the majority of variance, each containing faster movement/posture configuration vectors as substructure. Reconfigurations (measured as nonzero Hamming distances between binary action vectors) will correspond to crossings of potential barriers between metastable minima, and the exploratory breadth Q (average escape probability) will be significantly greater than zero, reflecting the system's creative fluency under relaxed constraints.
primary_finding: Analysis of a 450-second contact improvisation dance session revealed a soft-assembled hierarchical structure of movement dynamics consistent with the theoretical model. Hierarchical PCA identified 7 primary PCs accounting for 81.3% of total variance, with a secondary level of 2 PCs containing the 7 primary PCs as substructure, reducing 449 configuration vectors to two slowest collective variables. The median dwell time of configuration vectors was 5.4 seconds. The average dynamic overlap <qd(t)> showed a power-law initial relaxation (β = -0.203; R²=0.977) and formed a plateau at approximately <qd> = 0.45, confirming broken ergodicity and a non-equilibrium state consistent with soft-assembled hierarchical dynamics. The exploratory breadth Q = 0.37, showing significant exploratory capacity under relaxed constraints. These results confirm that creative behaviour in dance unfolds on multiple time scales due to the hierarchical structure of the perception-action landscape, with both process (exploration) and product (novel movement forms) aspects of creativity emerging from metastable attractor dynamics.
raw_quote: This modeling shows how exploration is a requisite of creative behaviour, i.e. inventing novel or innovating extant movement forms and actions with respect to the extant socio-cultural milieu (for details see Hristovski et al., 2011).

source_paper_id: 17. Unlocking Creative Movement with Inertial Technology – Sánchez Martz et al., 2025.pdf
paper_title: Unlocking Creative Movement with Inertial Technology
key_methodology: Amplitude (A) at time t: A_t = ||p_rh,t - p_co,t|| + ||p_lh,t - p_co,t|| + ||p_rf,t - p_co,t|| + ||p_lf,t - p_co,t||, the sum of Euclidean distances from each limb (right hand, left hand, right foot, left foot) to the centre of gravity p_co,t; ||·|| denotes Euclidean norm. Total movement (TM): TM = Σ_{t=2..n} (||p_rh,t-1 - p_rh,t|| + ||p_lh,t-1 - p_lh,t|| + ||p_rf,t-1 - p_rf,t|| + ||p_lf,t-1 - p_lf,t||), sum over frames of Euclidean step lengths for those four endpoints. Risk assumed (R) at time t: R_t = ||p_cop,t - p_pbf,t||, Euclidean distance between centre-of-gravity projection on the ground (COP) and posterior base of support (PBF, midpoint between feet). Units: centimetres. Normality: Shapiro–Wilk on A, TM, R. Paired comparisons Visual Representation (VR) vs Narrative Representation (NR): paired t-test for amplitude (normal); Wilcoxon signed-rank for total movement and risk (non-normal); α = 0.05. N = 24 professional dancers.
primary_finding: Amplitude: paired t(23) = 2.73, p = 0.012. Risk: Wilcoxon S = 92, p = 0.0057. Total movement: Wilcoxon S = 68, p = 0.0497. Descriptive means (cm, Table 2): A_VR mean 2.662 (SD 0.134, min 2.376, median 2.664, max 2.892); A2_NR mean 2.696 (SD 0.119, min 2.364, median 2.708, max 2.918). R1_VR mean 0.252 (SD 0.061, min 0.138, median 0.244, max 0.388); R2_NR mean 0.279 (SD 0.058, min 0.127, median 0.283, max 0.433). TM1_VR mean 219.620 (SD 79.167, min 69.080, median 232.460, max 357.050); TM2_NR mean 266.150 (SD 87.835, min 65.700, median 284.790, max 449.870). Increment NR−VR (Table 1): amplitude <0 in 6 (25.00%), >0 in 18 (75.00%); risk <0 in 5 (20.83%), >0 in 19 (79.17%); total movement <0 in 10 (45.83%), >0 in 14 (58.33%). Example mean increment in amplitude inc_a = 0.034 cm. Discussion counts: 18/24 higher amplitude, 19/24 higher risk, 14/24 higher total movement under narrative imagery.
raw_quote:
cant differences were found in amplitude ( t23 = 2.73, p = 0.012), risk ( Wilcoxon, S = 92,
p = 0.0057), and total movement (Wilcoxon, S = 68, p = 0.0497), indicating significant measur-
able effects of the condition on postural parameters (Table 2, Figure 2).

source_paper_id: 17. Unlocking Creative Movement with Inertial Technology – Sánchez Martz et al., 2025.pdf
paper_title: Unlocking Creative Movement with Inertial Technology
key_methodology: Visual Representation (VR, visual-episodic): dancers imagined a place tied to a specific experience and described that place through movement with emphasis on structural elements, object functions, and visual imagery. Narrative Representation (NR, narrative-episodic): the same place, but movement had to convey experiences and emotions linked to the space—communicating a narrative rather than depicting visual structure. Creative movement is framed theoretically as motor creativity within dance improvisation: discovering novel, purposeful ways to act adaptively (linked to movement variability), integrating internal episodic mental representations with first-person imagery and metaphor, and studying how cognitive simulation and sensorimotor execution jointly produce creative behaviour; the task operationalises this as generating original dance phrases under those two representation inductions.
primary_finding: The authors interpret narrative-episodic imagery (experiential and emotional narrative communication in movement) versus visual-episodic imagery (spatial/structural visual depiction) as engaging different cognitive representation content that propagates into motor execution during artistic creation: mental imagery is argued to exert a direct, representation-type-dependent influence on motor outcomes, such that the shift toward narrative content coincides with larger amplitude, more risk-taking, and greater overall movement volume—summarised as a significant impact of mental representations on motor execution within artistic processes related to creativity, and as evidence that narrative-based internal representations physically interact with the environment more expansively than visual-based ones in this paradigm.
raw_quote:
Accordingly, the primary
purpose of the study was to assess the effects of these distinct mental representations on
amplitude, risk-taking behavior, and overall movement quantity, using inertial sensor data
as an objective measure of postural and kinematic change.

paper_title: RQA Shannon entropy (ENTR) as a robust metric for movement variability
key_methodology: Recurrence Quantification Analysis (RQA) using pooled embedding (m=6, tau=8) and recurrence threshold epsilon=1.
primary_finding: RQA Shannon entropy (ENTR) is more robust for identifying individual differences in movement variability than REC, DET, or RATIO.
raw_quote: RQA Shannon entropy (ENTR) is highlighted as a robust RQA metric for individual differences in movement variability versus REC/DET/RATIO patterns in wrist-mounted sensor streams analyzed with pooled embedding m0=6, tau0=8 and recurrence threshold epsilon=1 (Figure 5 setting).

source_paper_id: JcvPCA and JsvCRP A set of metrics to evaluate changes in joint coordination strategies.pdf
paper_title: A set of metrics to evaluate changes in joint coordination strategies
key_methodology: JcvPCA (Joint Contribution Variation based on PCA): For datasets A (reference, k trials) and B (l trials), each trial stacks n joint trajectories θ; data are centered to zero mean but not range-normalized (to preserve relative joint contribution and limit noise amplification). PCA on A yields PCs PCA,u = Σ_i a_u,i θ_A,i (u = 1…m; authors suggest m = p+1 with p task DoF), defining frame R_A = {PCA,1…PCA,m}. Dataset B is projected per joint as θ^A_B,i = R_A θ_B,i; a second PCA on the projected trajectories gives PCB,u = Σ_i b_u,i θ^A_B,i so PCs are expressed in the same θ_A basis (Joint Reprojection Weight, JRW). JcvPCA per joint i and PC u is JcvPCA_u,i = |a_u,i| − |b^A_u,i| (optional reporting to explained variance: res_j = σ²_j × (PCB,u − PCA,u) with σ²_j the variance explained by each PC). JsvCRP (Joint Synchronization Variation based on CRP): Joint angle and velocity are range-normalized to [−1, 1] via θ_i,norm(t) = 2(θ_i(t) − θ_i,min)/(θ_i,max − θ_i,min) − 1 and analogous formula for θ̇; time normalization aligns CRP to 0–100% movement duration (e.g. simple scaling or DTW/registration when needed). Phase angle per joint is φ_i(θ_i, θ̇_i) = tan⁻¹(θ̇_i,norm(t)/θ_i,norm(t)) from the position–velocity phase portrait; pairwise CRP is CRP(θ_i, θ_j) = φ_j − φ_i. JsvCRP between conditions A and B for a joint pair is the L¹ area between mean CRP curves: JsvCRP_A,B = ∫_0^{t_movement} |CRP_B(θ_i, θ_j) − CRP_A(θ_i, θ_j)| dt (paper uses integral of absolute difference over movement duration). Validation used 2-DoF simulated sines and exoskeleton-recorded shoulder/elbow reaching with four strategies vs a physiological baseline; thresholds from random splits of the physiological condition.
primary_finding: JcvPCA and JsvCRP target complementary aspects: spatial distribution of joint contributions to shared PCs versus temporal phase synchronization. On simulated and experimental data, JcvPCA distinguished strategies that reweighted joint contributions to the task PCs (e.g. overuse of elbow, shoulder-only) while being weak for purely temporal desynchronization because PCA is largely insensitive to timing; JsvCRP separated temporal-desynchronization and shoulder-only conditions with large area metrics versus baseline natural-variability intervals, and showed smaller but above-threshold differences for elbow overuse where kinematic coupling was similar but timing differed at movement onsets/offsets. Together they differentiate coordination strategies that change how much each joint contributes from those that change lead–lag and phase coupling. Physiologically, a positive JcvPCA entry means the joint contributes more in B than in the reference A, negative means less—interpreted as over- or under-use of an axis relative to baseline, potentially reflecting compensation or altered control; JsvCRP magnitude scales with dissimilarity of continuous relative phase trajectories, i.e. how much inter-joint synchronization/phase coupling differs from the reference, with larger values indicating more distinct temporal coordination patterns (not interpretable in isolation without task context and natural-variability thresholds).
raw_quote:
In conclusion, the JsvCRP , defined as the area between 2 CRP curves, provides a valuable
indication of the extent of the changes in coordination strategy. A larger area between the
curves indicates a more substantial difference in the joint coordination patterns.

paper_title: Creativity in sport and dance: Ecological dynamics on a hierarchically soft-assembled perception-action landscape.
key_methodology: PART 1 — FORMAL QUANTITATIVE CASE: Actions/postures were coded on 52 coarse-grained movement/posture components (Torrents et al., 2010; Castañer et al., 2009); active components = 1, inactive = 0, yielding a binary matrix at 1 Hz. Each second is a 52-dimensional binary configuration vector. Reconfigurations are Hamming distances between successive (or arbitrary pairs of) binary vectors; a single 1↔0 flip has Hamming distance 1, interpreted as the height of the potential barrier between configurations. Overlap order parameter q uses cosine similarity and Pearson correlation between binary vectors; hPCA used the Pearson measure on binary data (Joliffe, 2002, p.339) to reveal nested attractor basin structure. Average dynamic overlap <qd(t)> is the average cosine auto-similarity of overlap between configurations as time lag increases. Theoretical exploratory breadth: Q = <We> with escape probability We = 1 − Wc, where Wc is the conditional probability of remaining in the same attractor (Hristovski et al., 2011; Saxton, 1996).
primary_finding: PART 2 — EMPIRICAL RESULTS (450 s contact improvisation): hPCA yielded 25 PCs; 7 primary PCs explained 81.3% variance; a secondary level of 2 PCs subsumed the 7 primaries, reducing 449 configuration vectors to two slow collective variables; median dwell time of configuration vectors 5.4 s. Dynamic overlap <qd(t)> showed non-exponential relaxation (distribution of relaxation times); log–log initial relaxation slope β = −0.203 (R² = 0.977); plateau mean overlap <qd> ≈ 0.45 for separations >35 s (ergodicity broken on the 7-min window). Reported exploratory breadth Q = 0.37 (average escape probability from a configuration under relaxed constraints).
raw_quote: Here we describe some results of an analysis of a typical contact improvisation session lasting 450 seconds under no special instructional constraints, except those provided by the contact with the partner, i.e. visual and haptic information, and the force of gravity. Sequences of actions/postures were analyzed to determine their complex dynamical characteristics. Actions/postures were defined on a coarse-grained scale containing 52 movement/posture components, such as support/contact characteristics and directions and planes of motion of body segments according to established observational methodology (see Torrents, et al, 2010; Castañer, et al, 2009). To the active components a value of 1 was ascribed and to the inactive components a value of 0. Hence, a binary matrix was formed with a time resolution of 1 second. Each 1 second window was defined as a 52-component binary vector representing the action configuration during the same time interval. Reconfigurations, i.e. mutations, of action patterns were calculated as Hamming distances between any two binary vectors. For example, the change of one component of the vector from 1 to 0 or vice versa has a Hamming distance equal to 1. Hence, the Hamming distance actually measures the height of the potential barrier between two configurations. Overlap order parameter q was used to determine the structure of the potential landscape of the dancer and its dynamic properties. The overlap was defined in two intrinsically-related association measures: as a cosine similarity and as a Pearson correlation between two binary configuration vectors. A hierarchical principal component analysis (hPCA) was performed on the data using the second measure (for the plausibility of using the PCA on binary variables see Joliffe, 2002, p.339) with the aim to detect the possible nested attractor basin structure of the dancer’s action landscape. The dynamic overlap <qd(t)> was calculated as an average cosine auto-similarity of the overlap between configurations with increasing time lag to determine the dynamic properties of dancer’s complex movement patterns.
